text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY -- MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD RE-EVALUATION AND FOLLOW-UP VISIT
Patient: Raymond Okafor | DOB: on file | MRN: on file
Attending: Medical Oncology
Participants: Medical Oncology, Hepatobiliary Surgery, Interventional Radiology, Colorectal Surgery, Radiology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT / REASON FOR VISIT
Mr. Okafor is a 58-year-old Nigerian-American male with stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases) presenting today for multidisciplinary tumor board re-evaluation following cycle 6 of first-line FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab, coinciding with restaging CT imaging obtained this week. The primary purpose of today's visit is to review the response assessment scan, discuss current toxicity management, and determine the optimal treatment plan for the next phase of therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS
Mr. Okafor was diagnosed approximately three months ago with stage IV adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases. At diagnosis, he had a 4.8 cm sigmoid colon primary with extension through the muscularis propria into the pericolorectal tissues and 3 of 18 regional lymph nodes involved. Hepatic disease included four lesions distributed across both lobes: segment II (1.4 cm), segment IVa (2.1 cm), segment VI (1.7 cm), and segment VII (3.2 cm as the dominant lesion). Comprehensive molecular profiling revealed a KRAS G12D-activating mutation, which categorically excluded him from anti-EGFR therapy. MSS status precluded checkpoint immunotherapy. The NGS also identified co-occurring TP53 R248W, APC R1450*, SMAD4 R361H (an adverse prognostic marker), PIK3CA E545K, and PTEN homozygous deletion, reflecting a genomically complex tumor.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion at diagnosis, the decision was made to pursue systemic induction chemotherapy with mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab as the first-line palliative-intent regimen, with the expressed goal of achieving sufficient response to potentially allow future liver-directed therapy, up to and including surgical resection. A totally implantable venous access device (Port-A-Cath) was placed prior to initiation.

Mr. Okafor completed cycles 1 through 6 of mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab on a biweekly schedule. Notable toxicities encountered included:

1. Peripheral sensory neuropathy: Developed grade 2 cumulative oxaliplatin-associated peripheral neuropathy after cycle 3, manifesting as numbness and tingling in bilateral hands and feet with some impairment of fine motor tasks but preserved activities of daily living. This was further complicated by his pre-existing baseline diabetic peripheral neuropathy (grade 1 at treatment initiation). Oxaliplatin was dose-reduced to 75 mg/m2 (from 85 mg/m2) per institutional guidelines after cycle 3. Neuropathy has remained at grade 2 and has not worsened since the dose reduction.

2. Bevacizumab-related hypertension: Blood pressure escalated to 162/98 mmHg at home readings after cycle 3, superimposed on his prior history of hypertension managed with lisinopril 10 mg daily. His antihypertensive regimen was intensified: lisinopril increased to 20 mg daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily was added. Blood pressure has since improved to below 140/90 mmHg.

3. Nausea: Grade 1, managed with prochlorperazine as needed.

Today's visit is prompted by the completion of the planned 6-cycle restaging interval. Mr. Okafor reports improved overall sense of well-being compared to his pre-treatment baseline. He is working part-time and remains active in a limited administrative capacity at his school. He endorses grade 2 neuropathy affecting both hands and feet as his primary complaint. He denies any new abdominal symptoms, change in bowel habits, rectal bleeding, or abdominal pain. He has had no symptoms suggestive of bowel obstruction. Mild fatigue (grade 1-2) persists, worse in the 3-4 days following infusion, and then improves. He denies significant nausea with the current cycle. No mouth sores, diarrhea, or rash.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS
Constitutional: Mild fatigue as noted above; no fevers, chills, or night sweats. No significant unintentional weight loss (weight stable within 2 kg of baseline).
Neurologic: Ongoing bilateral distal sensory neuropathy, hands and feet, grade 2. Tingling and numbness predominant; no significant pain component. Some difficulty with fine motor tasks (difficulty buttoning shirt, handling small objects). No new headache, visual changes, or focal neurologic deficits.
Cardiovascular: No chest pain or palpitations. Blood pressure well-controlled on current regimen. No lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: No cough, shortness of breath, or hemoptysis.
Gastrointestinal: No abdominal pain. No change in bowel habits. No hematochezia or melena. No nausea or vomiting at present. Appetite mildly reduced in the peri-infusion period but otherwise near baseline.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain or arthralgias.
Dermatologic: No hand-foot syndrome, rash, or skin changes.
Genitourinary: No dysuria. Proteinuria screening not yet formally re-evaluated this cycle (pending today's labs).
Endocrine/Metabolic: Diabetes mellitus type 2 -- blood glucose well-controlled on metformin per patient report; no hypoglycemic episodes.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
General: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented. Appears his stated age.
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 142/88 mmHg (left arm, seated), heart rate 74 bpm, respiratory rate 16, temperature 37.0 C, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Weight 84.2 kg (BMI approximately 28.2).
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No scleral icterus. Mucous membranes moist. No oral mucositis.
Neck: No cervical lymphadenopathy. No JVD.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. No palpable hepatomegaly or splenomegaly on clinical exam (liver edge not appreciated below costal margin). No palpable abdominal or pelvic masses. No tenderness to palpation. Sigmoid colectomy scar well-healed. Bowel sounds normal. No ascites on clinical exam.
Extremities: No lower extremity edema or calf tenderness. Port-A-Cath in right chest wall, site clean and non-tender, no surrounding erythema.
Neurologic: Cranial nerves intact. Decreased sensation to light touch in bilateral feet (to the mid-calf level) and bilateral fingertips/distal hands. Vibration sense diminished bilaterally in toes. Gait is mildly ataxic but patient is ambulatory without assistive device. Deep tendon reflexes diminished at bilateral ankles. No upper motor neuron signs.
Skin: No palmar-plantar erythema or blistering (no hand-foot syndrome). No rash.
ECOG Performance Status: 1.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS
CBC: WBC 5.8 x10^9/L, hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL (mild anemia, stable, likely anemia of chronic disease/cancer), platelets 282 x10^9/L, ANC 3.2 x10^9/L. No neutropenia.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.2 mEq/L, creatinine 0.98 mg/dL (eGFR >60), total bilirubin 0.8 mg/dL, ALT 32 U/L, AST 28 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 118 U/L (mild elevation, present at baseline, consistent with hepatic metastatic disease). Albumin 3.8 g/dL.
Coagulation: INR 1.1.
Tumor Markers: CEA 94.1 ng/mL (down from baseline 187.3 ng/mL -- approximately 50% reduction from baseline); CA 19-9 198 U/mL (down from baseline 412 U/mL -- approximately 52% reduction). [Note: These values are from the cycle 3 check approximately 2 months ago; today's values pending.]
Urinalysis: Urine protein trace (1+), no RBC or WBC. Spot protein/creatinine ratio 0.18 (below threshold for bevacizumab dose modification; will continue to monitor).
Blood glucose (fingerstick): 138 mg/dL (mild post-prandial elevation, within acceptable range for diabetes management).
HbA1c: 7.2% (drawn at last visit; acceptable glycemic control).

---

IMAGING RESULTS
Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (obtained this week, reviewed at today's multidisciplinary tumor board):

The sigmoid colon primary tumor has decreased from 4.8 cm at baseline to 3.1 cm -- a reduction of approximately 35% in maximal dimension. There remains pericolic fat stranding, though this has diminished compared to the staging scan. The sigmoid colon is patent without evidence of obstruction.

Hepatic lesions have all responded:
- Segment II lesion: decreased from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm
- Segment IVa lesion: decreased from 2.1 cm to 1.4 cm
- Segment VI lesion: decreased from 1.7 cm to 1.1 cm
- Segment VII lesion (dominant): decreased from 3.2 cm to 2.1 cm

Overall, the sum of hepatic target lesions has decreased from approximately 9.1 cm to 5.5 cm, representing roughly a 39-40% reduction. No new hepatic lesions have appeared. No biliary dilation.

No pulmonary nodules or masses. No pleural effusions. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy.

No peritoneal implants or ascites. No pelvic masses. No new osseous lesions.

Impression: Favorable radiographic response to FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab with meaningful shrinkage of both the sigmoid colon primary and all four hepatic metastases. This is consistent with a solid partial response by any metric. No new sites of disease.

Prior tumor markers at the cycle 3 check confirmed consistent response (CEA 94.1 ng/mL, down from 187.3 ng/mL; CA 19-9 198 U/mL, down from 412 U/mL). Today's labs will further confirm trajectory.

---

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION
All relevant disciplines reviewed the imaging in real-time. The following perspectives were offered:

HEPATOBILIARY SURGERY: Reviewed the updated CT images carefully. While all four lesions have decreased in size, the segment VII lesion at 2.1 cm remains in close proximity to the right hepatic vein and the junction of the right and middle hepatic veins. An R0 resection of the segment VII lesion at this time would require a challenging dissection with significant risk of positive margins or vascular injury. Additionally, the bilobar distribution of disease, involving segments II and IVa on the left lobe and segments VI and VII on the right, would likely require either a two-stage hepatectomy or a combined resection/ablation strategy to achieve complete treatment of all hepatic disease while preserving adequate hepatic parenchymal volume. Given the favorable ongoing response, the hepatobiliary surgery team strongly favors continued systemic therapy with reassessment at the 12-cycle mark. Specific concern: the SMAD4 mutation detected on molecular profiling is associated with hepatic-dominant metastatic spread and reduced likelihood of durable surgical control -- this is factored into the risk-benefit calculus.

INTERVENTIONAL RADIOLOGY: Segment VI lesion (currently 1.1 cm) is becoming more amenable to radiofrequency ablation or microwave ablation given its location and size. If it continues to decrease or stabilizes below 1.5 cm at cycle 12 reassessment, it could be treated percutaneously in a minimally invasive fashion, potentially allowing that lesion to be managed without open surgery. Segment II and IVa lesions are also potentially ablatable if they continue to respond. Segment VII remains the most technically challenging target given proximity to major vascular structures.

COLORECTAL SURGERY: The sigmoid colon primary at 3.1 cm remains unresected. No evidence of impending obstruction or perforation. Will reassess the primary at the 12-cycle mark; primary resection may be considered if hepatic disease is adequately controlled or if the patient is a potential conversion candidate.

MEDICAL ONCOLOGY: In the context of this excellent partial response with approximately 40% reduction in target lesion burden at just 6 cycles, continuing on the current FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab regimen is the most appropriate strategy. Oxaliplatin was dose-reduced to 75 mg/m2 after cycle 3 due to grade 2 peripheral neuropathy. We are monitoring neuropathy closely. The patient has had acceptable tolerability otherwise. We plan to continue through cycle 12 with reassessment, then reconvene the MDT for a definitive discussion about hepatic resection, staged resection/ablation, or other hepatic-directed strategies.

CONSENSUS DECISION: Continue FOLFOX6 (oxaliplatin at reduced dose 75 mg/m2) plus bevacizumab through cycle 12. Restaging imaging to be performed after cycle 12. Full multidisciplinary reassessment for hepatic surgery and/or ablation at that time. No changes to current supportive medications.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. STAGE IV SIGMOID COLON ADENOCARCINOMA (KRAS G12D-MUTANT, MSS), with bilobar hepatic metastases -- PARTIAL RESPONSE to first-line FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab after 6 cycles.
   - Continue mFOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab cycles 7 through 12 on biweekly schedule.
   - Oxaliplatin will remain at reduced dose of 75 mg/m2 (grade 2 neuropathy, dose reduction in effect).
   - All other components at current doses: leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus, 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours, bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV day 1.
   - Antiemetic prophylaxis with ondansetron and dexamethasone to continue.
   - Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast planned after cycle 12.
   - Full MDT re-evaluation at that time for discussion of hepatic-directed therapy options (resection vs. staged resection with ablation vs. ablation alone).
   - Patient counseled on continued palliative intent of current therapy and potential conversion to hepatic-directed therapy if response is maintained or deepened.

2. BEVACIZUMAB-RELATED AND PRE-EXISTING HYPERTENSION -- currently improved on lisinopril 20 mg daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily.
   - Blood pressure today 142/88 mmHg -- at goal.
   - Continue current antihypertensive regimen.
   - Will recheck blood pressure at next infusion visit.
   - Urine protein: trace on today's dip, spot protein/creatinine 0.18 -- below threshold; will continue to monitor at each visit. If proteinuria exceeds 2 g/24 hours or nephrotic syndrome develops, bevacizumab will be held per standard guidelines.

3. GRADE 2 PERIPHERAL SENSORY NEUROPATHY -- attributed to cumulative oxaliplatin exposure, compounded by pre-existing diabetic peripheral neuropathy.
   - Oxaliplatin dose reduction to 75 mg/m2 already in place.
   - Monitor neuropathy grade closely at each infusion visit. If neuropathy progresses to grade 3 (significant functional impairment, interference with activities of daily living), oxaliplatin will be discontinued entirely and patient transitioned to de-escalated 5-FU/leucovorin plus bevacizumab (oxaliplatin-stop strategy).
   - Patient counseled to report any worsening tingling, burning, new pain, or balance difficulty promptly.
   - Referral to physical therapy for gait and balance assessment considered given reported mild ataxia.

4. TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS -- well-controlled.
   - Continue metformin at current dose.
   - HbA1c 7.2% -- acceptable.
   - Dexamethasone steroid component of antiemetic regimen may transiently elevate blood glucose on infusion days; patient is aware and monitoring at home.
   - Renal function within normal limits (creatinine 0.98 mg/dL); metformin safe to continue.

5. MILD ANEMIA -- hemoglobin 11.9 g/dL.
   - Likely anemia of chronic disease in the setting of malignancy and ongoing chemotherapy.
   - No transfusion required at this time; patient is asymptomatic.
   - Iron studies to be checked at next visit to rule out iron deficiency component.
   - Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents not indicated at this time.

6. NAUSEA -- grade 1, managed with prochlorperazine PRN.
   - Continue as needed.
   - If nausea escalates, consider adding lorazepam or scopolamine for anticipatory nausea.

7. PORT-A-CATH -- right chest wall, functioning without complications.
   - Continue standard port care and flushing per nursing protocol.

8. GENERAL SUPPORTIVE CARE AND FOLLOW-UP:
   - Patient will follow up at infusion center every 14 days for next cycle of FOLFOX6 plus bevacizumab.
   - Routine labs (CBC, CMP, CEA, CA 19-9) to be checked prior to each cycle.
   - Next formal oncology clinic visit in approximately 4 weeks, or sooner if clinical concern arises.
   - Patient instructed on neuropathy monitoring and emergency signs (febrile neutropenia, severe diarrhea, uncontrolled vomiting, significant bleeding, sudden blood pressure elevation).
   - Continue gabapentin if needed for neuropathic discomfort (discussed as an option if pain component develops).

PROGNOSTIC DISCUSSION:
Mr. Okafor and his wife were present for today's visit. He was counseled again regarding the palliative intent of current therapy. The response we are seeing at 6 cycles is encouraging and represents a meaningful step toward the goal of conversion to potentially operable or ablatable hepatic disease. We discussed that the SMAD4 mutation in his tumor carries an adverse prognostic implication, particularly with regard to hepatic metastatic biology, and that our goal is to achieve maximum benefit from systemic therapy before committing to surgical or ablative intervention. He was reminded that the response needs to be maintained or deepened through cycle 12 for liver-directed therapy to be feasible. He expressed understanding and remains highly motivated. His wife asked about clinical trials; I explained that given his current response on standard first-line therapy, continued first-line treatment is the priority. We will revisit trial eligibility at the point of progression.

Time spent with patient: approximately 55 minutes including MDT conference review and patient counseling.
All questions addressed.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Medical Oncology
